generations.text:
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit before the flickering light of the oil lamp, the salt air thick with the scent of storm and memory. Five long years have passed since you left this tower, and I have not spoken a single word to you since. The sea has been my only companion, and the wind has been my only confidant. Yet tonight, as the heavens weep and the waves rise in fury, I find myself compelled to write—though I do not know if my voice will reach you, or if the sea will claim me first.

You were always a firebrand, Eliza. A spirit untamed by the rules of the world, and I—your father—was too proud to see the storm in your eyes. I told you to stay in the city, to marry well, to become a lady of means. But you chose the sea, and in your heart, you chose me. I was the lighthouse, the beacon, the guardian of the rocks. You were the storm, and I was the shore.

I have watched the horizon for years, waiting for your return. I have stood in the cold, the rain, the gales, and I have not once faltered. But the sea is cruel, and so is fate. I have seen the waves rise, and I have seen the cliffs crumble. I have seen the light of the lighthouse flicker, and I have seen the darkness of my heart

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit by the window of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit, the sea roaring with a fury that makes my very bones tremble. The lantern burns with a steady light, as it always has, and yet I feel it flicker in my heart, a fragile thing, like the fragile hope that I still hold for you.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and the silence between us has grown heavier than the stone walls of this tower. I have written to you, many times, but your replies have been fewer, and when they came, they were always brief, as if you could not bear the weight of my words. I do not blame you, Eliza. I know that time has been cruel to us both, and that the world has changed in ways I can scarcely comprehend.

I have grown older, and the sea has grown more cruel. The storms are fiercer, the waves more relentless, and the nights more long. I have come to know the language of the wind, the rhythm of the tides, and the patience required to keep the light burning through the darkest hours. It is a lonely life, but I have made peace with it, in part because I have not had to face the loneliness of a child's absence.

Tonight, however, the storm comes with a vengeance. The sky is black as ink, the air thick with the scent of brine and

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast outside and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear that even now, I may not have the strength to say much. But tonight, as the storm gathers, I find myself compelled to write—perhaps to leave something behind, or perhaps to say what I should have said long ago.

You were a child when I last saw you, no more than seven or eight, running along the shore with your little dog, chasing waves that never stayed. I remember how you laughed, how your eyes sparkled like the sea at dawn. You were my joy, my hope, the light that kept me going through the long, lonely nights. And yet, I let it fade. I let the years pass without a word, without a visit, without a single letter.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years now, and in that time, I have seen many storms. Some came with warning, others with a whisper. But none have ever felt as heavy as the silence between us. You once called me "Papa," and I used to think that was enough. But time, my dear, is a cruel thing. It does not forgive, nor does it forget. It only moves forward, and I have moved forward without you.

I do not know if

My dear [Daughter's Name],

I hope this letter finds you in good health. It has been far too long since we last correspond,, and I must confess I have been feeling rather melancholy of late. The sea has been restless of late, and I fear the storm that is coming may be the worst we have seen in many a year.

I trust you are well, and I hope you have not been too troubled by the silence. I have often thought of you, and I have missed your letters, though I know you have not written back. It is a cruel irony, I daresay, but I must be honest.

As the storm approaches, I have taken to preparing for the worst. I have made sure to secure the lighthouse, to ensure it will stand against the storm's fury. I have also made provisions for the keepers, to ensure they will be safe should the storm come upon us.

I have also made sure to secure the lantern, to ensure it will burn bright and steady in the darkness. I have not forgotten the light, though I fear it may be dimmed by the coming storm.

I have also made sure to secure the mirrors, to ensure they will reflect the light back to us, so that we may see the path of the storm and know where to go.

I have also made sure to secure the beacon, to ensure it will shine with a steady light, so that we may be guided through the storm.

I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee through the cliffs, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very stones upon which this lighthouse stands. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not end as it began. Yet I must write, for in this moment of uncertainty, the only thing I can hold onto is the hope that you might read these words and perhaps, just perhaps, feel something—something more than the cold indifference that has grown between us.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you walked out that door, your face a mask of sorrow, your eyes filled with something I could not name. I tried to reach you, to call you back, but the silence was thick as the fog that curls in from the sea. You never answered. And I never wrote again.

I have always been a man of duty, Eleanor. A man who believed in the light, in the purpose of this tower, in the responsibility I bore to those who sailed the waters beyond. But I have also been a man who held on to the past, to the warmth of a home I no longer had, to the love I no longer knew how to express.

You were my light, my daughter. And I let it go.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm rages and the waves crash against the cliffs like

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a heavy heart, my hands trembling not only from the chill of the wind that howls through the tower but from the weight of the silence that has stretched between us for five long years. You have not written, nor have I dared to send a single letter, for fear that even the ink might carry the wrong words, or worse, that I might not have the strength to say what needs to be said.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may be the last one I shall see. The sea has been restless these past few nights, and the sky has darkened with a brooding, ominous grey. I’ve been watching the waves grow more furious, as though they too know of the sorrow that has been building in my soul. It is not the sea alone that has been storming; it is my heart, and I have no one to confide in but the wind and the waves.

I know you do not want to hear from me. You have every reason to be angry, to be distant, to be cut off from the man who once held you close in his arms. I was not always the kind of father you deserved, and I fear I have only ever been the kind of man who kept his head down, his hands busy with the lamp and the log, and his heart locked away in a place where no one could reach it.

You were a child once, and I was a man

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken, and the knowledge that time, like the sea, is fickle and cruel. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt your presence in the quiet corners of this lighthouse. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, for the sailors who depend on it, but I have not kept the light of your love, nor the warmth of your company.

The storm is coming, and it is not the kind that merely shakes the windows or cracks the stone. It is a tempest of wind and fury, one that has been foretold in the whispers of the sea and the shifting of the stars. The waves rise like the fury of a thousand souls, and the wind howls as if it carries the voices of the lost. I have prepared the lantern, and I have lit the oil, but I fear the light may not be enough to save us all.

I have always believed that the sea is a cruel mistress, but I never thought it would take you from me. You left, Eleanor, with words that cut deeper than any blade, and I have carried the silence like a burden. I have told myself that it was for your own good, that you would find a life beyond this place, beyond the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink of a trembling hand, the lamp in my tower flickering like the last breath of a dying man. The wind howls outside, a sound that echoes with the weight of years unspoken. Five years, Eliza. Five years since the last letter you sent me, and five since I last saw your face. The sea, as ever, is a cruel mistress, and the storm that now gathers on the horizon is no mere tempest—it is the reckoning of time, the hush before the tide swallows all.

I have kept the light burning, as I have always done, but tonight, the waves grow louder, and the sky weeps. The lantern’s glass is cracked, and the oil is low. I have no more strength to tend it, and the wind has begun to speak in voices I do not recognize. It sings of you, I think, or perhaps it is only the sea, ancient and unrelenting, calling to the bones of the earth.

You were always a child of the storm, Eliza. I remember the nights you stood on the cliffs, barefoot and wild, as if the wind itself had made you its own. You spoke of the sea as if it were a living thing, and I, a fool, believed you. But then came the day you left, and with it, the silence. Not the silence of absence, but of something deeper

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here by the window, the wind howling like a wounded beast, the sea roiling in its fury. The lantern flickers, and I can scarcely keep the light steady, though I know it must be. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just the lighthouse.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not forgotten the last words we exchanged. You told me I was a fool, that I had chosen this life over the one you wished for me. I do not deny it. I have chosen this life, and I have no regrets. But I have regretted the silence that followed.

I have watched the world change from my tower. The world you once knew is gone, replaced by machines and men who speak of progress, of industry, of the future. I have seen the ships pass by, their sails tattered, their crews weary, and I have wondered if you would still be there, in the city, chasing your dreams. I wonder if you ever thought of me.

You were always so full of fire, Eleanor. I remember the way you used to light up the room, your voice so full of life, your eyes so bright. I used to wish I could have been the one to guide you, to protect you. But I was not. I was a man who chose the sea over the world, and I have lived with that choice ever since.

Now

**April 12th, 1887**  
**The Lighthouse, North Sea Coast**  
**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the window, the wind howling like a wounded beast, and I think of you—my Eleanor, my darling. Five years have passed since you left this house, and yet I still feel your presence in the air, in the creak of the floorboards, in the silence of the night. The sea has been restless these past few days, and I fear the storm that's coming may be the last I see of this world.

You remember the old tales we used to tell, the ones about the lighthouse and the sea and the power of the light to guide the lost. Well, I've been the keeper here for twenty years now, and I've seen more storms than I can count. But tonight... tonight feels different. The sky is black as a thundercloud, and the wind is carrying with it a sound like the moaning of the dead. I've lit the lamp, and I've prayed, and I've held my breath, waiting for the end. But I don't know if I'll make it through the night, and I don't know if I'll see sunrise.

I've thought of you often, Eleanor. I've thought of the days we used to spend in the garden, the way you'd laugh as the bees buzzed around us, and the way you'd

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, by the flickering light of the lighthouse’s oil lamp, as the wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls. The sea roars in its fury, and I can only hope that the waves will not claim me before I can finish these words. You may never receive them, but I must try.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and yet it feels like a lifetime. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt your touch. The silence between us has grown heavier with each passing day, like the weight of the storm that now gathers on the horizon. I have tried, Eliza, I have truly tried, to be the father you deserved. But the sea has always been a cruel teacher, and I fear that I have become more its servant than a man of flesh and blood.

You were young when you left, and I was too proud to ask you to stay. I thought that if I gave you space, you would find your way back. But you did not. You left, and you have not returned. I have not written, nor have I dared to call. I have let the years pass like the tides, indifferent and relentless.

But tonight is different. Tonight, the sky is dark with clouds, and the wind is not just a whisper—it is a scream. I have watched the sea for decades, and I know when it

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of a weary soul and the quill of a man who has long since given up on the things that once mattered most. Five years have passed since the last time our hands met, and the silence between us has grown as thick as the fog that clings to these rocks at dawn. I have not spoken to you, and I have not written. But tonight, as the wind howls like a beast from the sea and the sky darkens with a storm I fear may not pass, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

You were once my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the lighthouse, but the light of my heart, the warmth that made this lonely life bearable. You were the daughter I held in my arms when I first took this post, the child who taught me the meaning of love, of patience, of hope. And then, without warning, you left—like the tide retreating from the shore, leaving behind only the echo of your laughter and the silence of your absence.

I do not know what drove you away. I do not know if it was your own heart, or the world, or something I failed to see. But I have not asked. I have not tried. Because I have been afraid that if I did, I might not be able to stop myself from saying the things I should not, from pleading, from begging, from begging for your return.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the cries of a damned soul, and the sea roiling as if it seeks vengeance upon the very earth. Five years have passed since you left this tower, and in that time, I have grown accustomed to the silence. But tonight—tonight—there is a storm that will not let us rest, and I fear the end may come before the dawn.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor, and yet I find myself yearning for your voice, your presence, as if it were the very breath of life. I have tended to this lighthouse, this sentinel of the sea, with unwavering devotion, but I have not tended to my own heart. It has grown cold and brittle, like the stone walls that guard us from the tempest.

You once said that you could not stay here, that the sea was too wild, that the loneliness was too great. I remember your words, and I told you that the sea was wild, yes, but it was also beautiful, and the loneliness... it was a companion, a companion that taught me patience and endurance.

But now, I wonder if you were right. If the sea was too wild, if the loneliness was too great, then why did you leave? Did you think I would not understand? Did you think I would not miss you? I have missed you every day, every night,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with a fury that might shake the very bones of this old lighthouse. The storm is coming, and I am not certain I shall see dawn. But if I do, I shall not be the same man I was when I last saw you, and I fear that even if I survive, I may not be the man you wish to see.

Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I have tried, God forgive me, to forget the way your voice filled this house with warmth and light. I have tried to forget the way your eyes, so full of mischief and grace, would meet mine with a kindness that made me feel both a fool and a king. But the sea is not a kind mistress, and the years have been cruel. I have grown hard, like the stone walls of this lighthouse, and I fear that you have grown tired of waiting for a man who has not known how to love you properly.

I remember the day you left. It was a quiet morning, the kind that makes you believe the world is at peace. I watched you from the window as you walked down the path to the ferry, your cloak flapping like the wings of a bird, and I did not call you back. I told myself I was doing the right thing. I told myself you would be better off without me. But

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the flicker of a single lantern in the dark, its light barely piercing the gathering storm. The sea roars like a beast in pain, and the wind howls as though it mourns. I have been here for many years, watching the waves crash against the rocks, and yet tonight feels different. The sky is heavy with black clouds, and the air is thick with the scent of salt and impending ruin. I fear that this may be the night I do not rise again.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Five long years. I do not know if you remember me, or if you have forgotten the man who once held you in his arms when you were a child. I was your father, and I failed you. I failed to be the man you needed. I let the sea take me away, and I let the world turn its back on me. I did not write, I did not call, and I did not return. I let the silence grow between us, thick as the fog that clings to the rocks at dawn.

But tonight is different. I have no more regrets, and I have no more time. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I will see the dawn. If I do not, I wish you to know that I have never stopped loving you. I have always loved you, even when I was too proud to say it. Even

I hope this letter finds you well. It has been far too long since we last spoke, and I must confess I have been quite preoccupied with the duties of this lighthouse. I have been keeping a close watch on the horizon, and I daresay I have not been entirely unsuccessful in my endeavors to keep the light burning.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have tried to carry on, I find myself longing for your voice, your laughter, and the warmth of your presence. I have written many letters in that time, but none have ever left my hands, and I suppose it is because I have never felt the right moment to send them.

The nights have grown colder, and the sea, once a companion, now seems a cruel and unyielding force. I have seen many storms in my years at the lighthouse, but none like this one. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you this evening, with the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, and the sea roiling as if it too knows of the sorrow that has long settled in my heart. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn. But if I do, I must tell you this—*I have never stopped thinking of you*.

Five years have passed since you left, and though I have not heard from you, I have not forgotten. You were but a child when you walked out that door, and though your words were sharp and your heart was heavy, I could not bear to chase you. Not then. And not now.

I have spent these years tending to the lighthouse, watching the tides, and keeping the light burning for those who sail the dark waters. It is a lonely duty, but it is one I have chosen, and in its quiet solitude, I have found solace in the thought that perhaps, one day, you might return. I have kept your portrait on the wall above the lantern, and every night, I trace the lines of your face with my eyes, wondering what path you have taken and if you still remember the girl who once called me "Papa."

I know you do not believe I am worthy of your forgiveness, and I cannot blame you. I was a man who failed to see the worth in the love you gave me

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12, 1893**

**Dear Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since your mother passed, and five more since you left this house, this life, and this father behind. I have not written to you, nor spoken of you, for fear that my words would not be enough to mend what has been broken. But tonight, as the wind howls through the cliffs and the sea roars like a beast awakened from its slumber, I find myself unable to hold my tongue any longer.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. It is not the kind of storm that merely shakes the windows or drowns the lantern. It is a storm that whispers of the end, and I fear it may come too soon for me. I have kept this light burning for thirty years, through wars, through the deaths of men and women I once knew, through the quiet grief of a lighthouse keeper’s life. But tonight, I feel the weight of the world pressing against the glass, and I wonder if I will be the one to go before the light goes out.

I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if I ever will. You left in a rage, with your mother’s death still fresh in your heart, and with the words of a man who had once been your father—words you chose to ignore. I have tried to forgive you, Eleanor, though I have

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles, not from the cold, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years since we last exchanged a word, and still I find myself haunted by the echo of your laughter in the hollows of this lighthouse. The sea, as ever, is restless, and the storm that brews on the horizon is no mere squall—it is a thing of teeth and wrath, a reckoning I do not believe I shall survive.

You were always the light in my darkness, Eleanor. You brought me books, and tea, and the faintest hope that perhaps, somewhere beyond this stone and salt, there was a world that still believed in love. I have kept the lamp burning for you, even when the wind howled like a banshee and the waves clawed at the cliffs with the fury of forgotten gods. I have watched the hours slip like sand through my fingers, and yet I have not turned the lamp out. You were worth it.

*My Dearest Eleanor,*

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has long since grown cold. Five years have passed since you last set foot in this lighthouse, and yet the weight of your absence has never felt heavier. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not written to you in five years. But tonight, as the storm gathers on the horizon, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the tempest within my soul. I have spent my life guarding this beacon, ensuring that no soul is lost to the cruel embrace of the Atlantic. But tonight, I fear I may not be able to guard myself. The storm is unlike any I have seen in my years of service, and I do not know if I will survive it.

I have always believed that the sea was a cruel mistress, but I never thought she would take such a toll on my own heart. You were my light in the darkness, my solace when the world felt too vast and cruel. And now, as I sit here in this lighthouse, I am forced to confront the truth that I have long buried: I have been alone for far too long.

**October 12th, 1887**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear I may not be the one to write the next. The sea has been restless these past few weeks, and the wind has grown colder than the heart of a man who has long since turned his back on the only family he ever knew. But tonight—tonight, my dear—there is a storm brewing that will test the very stones of this lighthouse, and I fear the sea may take more than it gives.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. Five long, lonely years. I have not written, nor have I sent a single letter, for I could not bear the thought of your eyes upon the page, or your voice in my mind. I have kept the lighthouse, as you once told me I should, and I have made a life of it, though it is not the life I would have chosen if I had known what it would cost. You were right, as you always were, in your own way.

I do not know if you will believe me when I say this, but I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the way you used to sit by the hearth in our old home, with your books and your tea, and the way you would look up at me with those eyes that saw through me as though I were a child

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and I find myself now, on this bleak and storm-laden night, more alone than I ever believed possible. The sea roars with a fury I have long grown accustomed to, but tonight it feels as though it is not merely the waves that threaten me, but the weight of time and regret.

You were once my greatest joy, my light in the darkest hours. I remember the day you left—your eyes filled with a fire that I could not understand, your voice sharp with words I could not unhear. I tried to reach you, to call you back, but the distance between us was not only the miles, but the silence that grew like ivy on the walls of our hearts. You called me a fool, and perhaps you were right. I was a fool to believe that love could be kept in a bottle and carried across the sea without spilling.

Tonight, the storm has come. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, once a steady companion, now rages with a fury that makes me fear for my life. I have spent my years watching the horizon, guiding ships through the dark, but never have I felt so lost as I do now. The light from the lighthouse flickers, uncertain, as if the very world is holding its breath.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**Lighthouse, Skerry Point**

I write this with the sea howling outside my window, the wind clawing at the shutters and the waves crashing against the rocks like the very soul of the sea is in revolt. I have watched the tides rise and fall, the stars blink above me, and the moon wane like a lover’s promise. Yet, I have not seen your face, nor heard your voice, nor felt your touch. Time, it seems, has carved a gulf between us, deeper than the ocean itself.

I know not what has driven you away, nor do I dare ask. Perhaps you think me a fool, or worse, a tyrant. I have been a father to you, a guardian of this place, and yet, I have been a stranger to you. I have failed you, Eliza, in the only way that matters—by not being there when you needed me most.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I write it with a heavy heart, knowing that it may never reach you. I have not spoken to you in five long years, and yet, the silence between us feels heavier than the stones that line the cliffs of this lonely coast.

It has been a cruel winter, and the sea has raged with a fury that I have never seen before. The wind howls through the tower like a banshee, and the waves crash against the rocks with a sound that echoes like a warning. Tonight, a storm is coming—one that may not pass, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be steadfast, that he must stand firm in the face of the elements, guiding the ships that pass in the dark. But now, as I look out over the sea, I am not so sure. I have done my duty, as I always have, but I wonder if I have done enough. I wonder if I have done right by you.

You were never meant to be a child of the sea, Eleanor. You were meant to be a child of the hearth, of music and poetry, of quiet evenings and warm fires. I tried to give you that. I tried to give you the world. But you chose your own path, and I could not follow. I could not understand.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not reach you in time. Five long years have passed since I last laid eyes upon you, and with each passing day, the weight of silence grows heavier on my heart. I write to you now not with the hope of rekindling what was lost, but with the need to speak, to be heard, and perhaps—perhaps—there is still a chance for us to mend what time and distance have broken.

The storm is coming, my girl. It has been gathering strength all day, a furious tempest that howls like the sorrow I have carried in my chest. The sea roars and the wind screams, and I am not certain I will see dawn. But if I do, I would not want to leave this world without one last word to you.

You were always my light, Eliza. Even when you were not here, you were in my thoughts, in the glow of the lantern, in the rhythm of the waves. I have kept the light burning every night, not just for the ships that pass, but for you. I have told myself that if you ever came back, I would still be here, waiting, just as the lighthouse stands, steadfast and unyielding.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*October 12, 1887*  
*The Lantern Room, White崖 Lighthouse, Isle of Skye*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the ink still wet, my hands trembling not from the cold, but from the weight of what I must say. The wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars with a fury that would make even the most hardened sailor weep. I have lit the lantern, as I have done for thirty years, and though the light burns bright, it cannot pierce the darkness within me.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of a heart that has grown heavier with each passing season. I have tried to forget, to bury the memories of your last words beneath the stones of this lonely keep. But the storm tonight has stirred something in me—something I have not allowed to surface in all this time.

You were never cruel, Eleanor. You were never the villain of your own story. You left because you believed, with all the fire in your young soul, that I was not the man you needed me to be. And perhaps, in your heart, you were right. I have been a man of duty, of duty alone. I have kept the light burning, but I have not kept the light of love in my own home.

**To My Dearest Child,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the leaden weight of the storm that brews beyond the cliffs. Five years have passed since you left, and though the lighthouse still stands, it feels as though the very stones have turned their backs on me. The sea, once a faithful companion, now roars with a fury I cannot name, and the wind howls as though it remembers your laughter, which it does not.

You were always a restless soul, my child. I recall the day you first asked to leave, your voice a whisper against the salt-laden air. "I must see the world, Father," you said, and I did not understand. I told you the sea would swallow you whole, that the light must be kept, that the night is a beast that must be tamed. But you were gone before I could finish my words.

And now, as the tempest gathers, I find myself questioning whether I have been a fool all along. The lantern burns with a dim, flickering light, as if it, too, is afraid. I have been alone for so long that I have forgotten the sound of your voice, yet I swear I hear it in the creak of the beam, in the groan of the tower, in the whisper of the wind that carries the scent of your childhood—of the sea salt and the bread you used to bake with me.

**April 12th, 1893**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow, for I fear the night shall not see me through. The storm brews on the horizon, dark as the devil's own shadow, and the sea howls like a wounded beast. I've lit the lamp, though I do not know if it will hold against the gales. The wind has grown so fierce, it seems to claw at the very stones of this tower.
